description:
You are an infrastructure engineer who wants to demonstrate that a freshly-provisioned host can
    1) install a tiny configuration database,  
    2) pre-populate it with a handful of known servers, and  
    3) export a quick status report that downstream tooling can parse automatically.  

    Use only the SQLite CLI program (`sqlite3`) that is already available in the container.  
    All work must happen inside the directory:

        /home/user/provisioning/db

    Desired final state of the system
    ---------------------------------
    1. Directory structure  
       • /home/user/provisioning/db/            – must exist (create any missing parents).  
       • The directory should remain world-readable but only user-writable (chmod 755 is fine).  

    2. SQLite database file  
       • Path: /home/user/provisioning/db/infra.db  
       • It shall contain exactly one table, named `servers`, created with:  
            id       INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,  
            hostname TEXT    NOT NULL,  
            ip       TEXT    NOT NULL,  
            status   TEXT    NOT NULL  
       • After your script finishes, the table must hold the three rows shown below (and *no* others):
            (1, 'web01',  '10.0.0.11', 'active')
            (2, 'db01',   '10.0.0.21', 'active')
            (3, 'cache1', '10.0.0.31', 'standby')

    3. Text report file  
       • Path: /home/user/provisioning/db/servers_report.log  
       • The report is the output of the following query **exactly as produced by the sqlite3 CLI in
         its default “list” mode** (pipe-separated, no header line, no extra whitespace):

            SELECT id,hostname,ip,status FROM servers ORDER BY id;

       • The file must therefore contain **exactly** these three lines (newline-separated, no trailing blank line):
            1|web01|10.0.0.11|active
            2|db01|10.0.0.21|active
            3|cache1|10.0.0.31|standby

         NOTE: The automated checker will do a byte-for-byte comparison; avoid extra spaces or line
         endings other than the single UNIX newline (`\n`) at the end of each line.

    4. Verification expectations  
       • The test harness will open /home/user/provisioning/db/infra.db with the `sqlite3` tool and
         run `SELECT * FROM servers;` — it must return only the three rows above.  
       • It will also run `cat /home/user/provisioning/db/servers_report.log` and compare its content
         exactly to the three-line block shown in point 3.

    Constraints / hints
    -------------------
    • You do NOT need root privileges.  
    • Keep the number of SQLite CLI invocations minimal (a single multi-line input file or interactive
      session is acceptable).  
    • Do not leave temporary files behind except those explicitly required above.  
    • Remember to create the parent directory tree before writing files inside it.  
    • All commands must be runnable in a plain bash shell on a typical Linux distribution with
      `sqlite3` already present.

    Summary of artefacts that **must exist at the end**:
      - /home/user/provisioning/db/infra.db        (SQLite database with populated `servers` table)
      - /home/user/provisioning/db/servers_report.log  (exact query output)

    No other files are required for the automated check.
truth:
1|web01|10.0.0.11|active2|db01|10.0.0.21|active3|cache1|10.0.0.31|standby
 1|web01|10.0.0.11|active 2|db01|10.0.0.21|active 3|cache1|10.0.0.31|standby
